data.title: IYR: Betting On REITs' Recovery
data.description:
I give a buy rating to the iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (IYR). The fund has a solid portfolio: ⅓ of it is positioned to benefit from megatrends, and the remaining ⅔ aimed at stability.

The REIT market is coming out of a difficult environment and is poised to recover, IYR is a good option to benefit from the upcoming cycle.

A Favorable Macroeconomic Environment

The macroeconomic environment, having been tough on real estate, will turn out to be more favorable.

For example, interest rates are projected to be cut by the end of the year, even if they are not expected to return to pre-pandemic levels. For the REIT industry, it means lower borrowing costs, and an increased projection for operating income growth: +3% for 2025, with a positive outlook of +6% for 2026.

Consumer spending has proven to be resilient, growing despite the uncertainty, and the unemployment rate remains close to historical lows, even if slightly growing. Geopolitical tensions and the tariff environment add uncertainty, but the economy appears to be resilient, and the recession risk that was on everyone's lips seems now to be far away.

This is a good picture for real estate, and I expect it will create an environment supportive of investments, perhaps even the beginning of a new growth cycle. One element to look forward to is the coming supply of secondary opportunities as over 1,700 midsize and smaller funds are maturing over the next couple of years, meaning there is an important opportunity for large asset managers looking to consolidate their portfolio. Indeed, secondary opportunities like that mean cheaper assets of better quality.

The industry actors are optimistic about the profitability, more than in 2024, and this is driven by prime assets. For the coming months, it will be crucial to prepare your portfolio with only top-tier assets, without looking for less appealing assets selling higher growth opportunities.

Megatrends: Digital Infrastructure and Others

In addition to the favorable macroeconomic environment above, 3 megatrends are bringing sources of upside, I will elaborate below.

AI and cloud computing

Data centers are a trendy topic now, as the industry is growing really fast under the AI growth. As of 2025, data center earnings are expected to grow by more than 20%. The market for data centers, now worth $270 Bn, will reach $585 Bn in 2032, exhibiting a CAGR of 11.7%.

This is clear when seeing that currently, there is a shortage of data center supply, and this is a strong tailwind pushing rents higher and stimulating investments, for example:

  • Amazon: Pledged $100 Bn towards AI data centers.

  • Alphabet: Committed $75 Bn.

  • Meta: Planning to spend up to $65 Bn.

  • Microsoft: $80 Bn in 2025 for AI data centers.

  • DAMAC Properties: $20 Bn investment in US data centers.

  • Taiwan Semiconductor: Announced $100 Bn investment in manufacturing facilities in the US.

5G and edge computing

The rollout of 5G is resulting in higher needs for telecom tower infrastructure, also the increasing mobile data consumption is adding to the mix. The market is projected to grow at a 6% CAGR to reach a size of $54 Bn in 2032. This market is very consolidated, and it is easy to leverage its growth because the market is only split among a few companies.

Industrial real estate

In this field, I especially mean logistics and warehousing, because of (i) e-commerce growth and (ii) supply chain reshoring. Because of these trends, the two markets are growing fast, even faster than the telecom tower one: the logistics market is projected to grow 7.2% from 2024 to 2030, and the warehousing market is projected to grow 8.1% from 2024 to 2030.

Health care REITs (bonus)

As business models are shifting and the industry is not exactly stable now, I will not include it in the 3 megatrends I mentioned, but I think there is a huge opportunity to be seized here because of the aging population and the shortages that the market is seeing.

I believe a portfolio holding REITs focusing on these fields has strong growth prospects.

Leveraging Megatrends with Balanced Exposure

IYR is tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Real Estate Capped Index. The fund has a 48.88% top 10 concentration, with half of it coming from the top 3 holdings: American Tower (AMT), Prologis (PLD) and Welltower (WELL). This is a high concentration, but compared to other leading REIT ETFs like Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) and its 51.27% top 10 concentration and The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR® Fund (XLRE) top 10 concentration at 62.51%, it actually is the most diversified option.

Megatrends holdings

IYR has a 12.48% stake in telecom tower REITs with holdings like AMT and Crown Castle (CCI), directly aligned with the 5G and edge computing megatrend. AMT is leading in wireless infrastructure, meaning IYR has a direct hold of the $54 Bn market growing by 2032.

The fund also has a 7.85% stake in data centers with holdings like Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty Trust (DLR), targeting AI and cloud computing megatrends. This is a crowded market with a lot of competition, but the current shortage of supply represents a strong short-term positioning.

Finally, the fund has a 10.40% industrial REIT stake, with PLD as the key holding, directly aimed at the logistics and warehousing market megatrend.

One third of the portfolio is positioned for growth through megatrends, let’s look at the rest of the portfolio.

Non-Megatrend Exposures

The fund has a 13.52% exposure to health care REIT, with WELL as the main one. This is a defensive stake that also benefits from strong trends I mentioned above. I think this sector gives the right mix of growth and stability.

Next, we have retail REITs with 12.59% of the portfolio, benefiting from strong macroeconomic indicators like resilient consumer spending. The market has proven to be resilient in front of e-commerce growth, however, the retail REIT market is especially driven by prime assets.

Smaller sectors in the portfolio, like self-storage (6.39%) with Extra Space Storage (EXR) is bringing stability thanks to the consistent consumer demand; multi- and single-family residential REITs (8.05%+4.21%) are benefiting from the potential recovery of the market and from demographic shifts. Additionally, the 1.95% stake in timber REITs and 2.74% in mortgage REITs are adding niche sectors with uncorrelated growth to our megatrends.

Financial Metrics

IYR is a relatively expensive ETF compared to peers, with an expense ratio of 0.38% against 0.13% and 0.08% for peers. The fund manages $3.8 Bn AUM, less than its two peers, but benefits from a higher daily trading volume of $765.8 Mn. I found this figure to be impressively high and went to look at the fund’s inflows and outflows, as expected, there are a lot of movements both ways:

This doesn’t hurt the fund’s bid-ask spread, below 0.01%.

The fund has a 2.45% dividend yield backed by a 30-day SEC yield of 2.80%, so it is very sustainable and has room for growth to catch up with peers offering higher dividend yields of 3.82% and 3.31%.

Looking at valuation metrics, the reporting of the three funds is not entirely consistent, as IYR doesn’t report on the P/E ratio and VNQ doesn’t report on its P/CF ratio. However, looking at the P/B ratio, IYR appears to be in the norm, slightly more valued than VNQ but less than XLRE.

Now let’s turn to the historical performance:

Reflecting on the difficulties of the REIT industry, the fund’s returns, just like for its peers, is not phenomenal. IYR is overperforming VNQ over the 1Y to 3Y period, but VNQ did better on longer time frames. On the other hand, XLRE consistently beat the two other funds. The past performance is not a big concern for me here, as I am betting on the recovery of the industry after the difficult past few years.

Risks

The 22.43% top 3 concentration, while being lower than peers, still strongly ties the fund’s performance with that of its main holdings, AMT, PLD and WELL. This also introduces a significant number of company-specific risks. For example, it can be the regulatory scrutiny over AMT's leading position or the supply chain problematics of PLD

Macroeconomic risks are also strong, as one of the key drivers of the recovery of REITs will be the rate cuts expected by the end of the year. If they are delayed, it would hurt the fund’s performance by deteriorating the growth potential of portfolio companies.

I will put a risk on the fund’s liquidity despite the good bid-ask spread considering the high inflows/outflows volatility.

Naturally, I don’t expect IYR to be exposed to significant risks due to the strength of its portfolio, but it is also dependent on a bet I’m making on the recovery of the real estate sector. If it doesn’t recover as fast as I expect, it will delay the fund’s returns.

Conclusion

I believe that the iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF is a strong, diversified REIT investment. I am bullish on the industry in general as I like its risk/reward ratio, and I also like the fund’s positioning, leveraging hot sectors for growth and more stable ones to protect capital.

data.title: Crispr Therapeutics: Sell Before It's Too Late
data.description:
CRISPR: Is The Selloff Really Over?

I guess that's what biotech investors should be familiar with, as the stock of CRISPR Therapeutics AG (CRSP) has truly undergone a roller coaster ride all year, after having bottomed out in April 2025. A surge in buying momentum appears to have sounded the death knell for bearish investors in June, as CRSP broke through several critical resistance zones to re-test the $70 level in July. While the buying sizzle has cooled off somewhat, the fact that CRSP's YTD return is almost up 40%, outperforming its healthcare sector peers (XLV), is notable.

Hence, could we really have reached the worst of the selloff in CRSP as investors contemplate whether the market reaction was too negative in the earlier part of the year? As a quick recap, I downgraded CRSP in my last update. While I acknowledge that CRISPR bulls have arguably proved that my timing was wrong since then, I urge the very same bulls not to get carried away with their optimism just yet. Why?

CRISPR: Another Five Years Before Possible Free Cash Flow Profitability

As a matter of fact, CRISPR's gene editing bullish narrative still relies on a pair of approvals, mainly on Casgevy for sickle cell disease, or SCD, and transfusion-dependent beta thalassemia, or TDT. But, I don't think I need to remind you further that these aren't new developments, even though the company appears to be making progress to address the potential opportunities across the global market.

Based on CRISPR's Q2 highlights for Casgevy, the company continues to make progress across its 75 authorized treatment centers worldwide. In the recent update, management indicated that "29 patients have received infusions of Casgevy, including 16 infused in the second quarter." Hence, I can't justifiably say that there's no material development for investors in CRISPR, with in vivo editing potentially expanding that addressable market and also lowering the cost barrier while allowing it to scale its business model.

In addition, the company's liquidity update for Q2 shows a reduction in cash and equivalents to $1.72B, down from $1.9B in December 2024. However, I think the market's worries about an unsustainable cash burn over the next five years should be less of a brouhaha.

Based on the updated estimates, CRISPR is anticipated to burn through more than $900M between 2025 and 2028 before turning free cash flow profitable in FY2029. Hence, CRISPR should have enough liquidity to overcome the near-term cash burn, helping to allay fears over its ability to pave its way to reaching FCF profitability.

CRISPR: Credit Must Be Given For Casgevy Progress In 2025

While I think these estimates should be regarded with significant ambiguities, the fact that noteworthy progress has been achieved by CRSP in 2025 to broaden its market access cannot be ignored. Hence, I think it might have afforded investors higher confidence that previous concerns about its ability to scale (including having securing the necessary manufacturing capacity/capabilities and distribution footprint) could have been overstated.

Yet bearish investors against CRSP haven't thrown in the white towel just yet. Based on its short interest ratio surpassing the 27% level at the end of July, the tide could still turn adversely against CRSP bulls, who added feverishly through the stock's momentum spike last month.

Even Morningstar reminded us that it has assigned "low probabilities" to the CRISPR's seemingly wide pipeline prospects (across cardiovascular, solid tumors, and Type 1 diabetes), as they are still mired in the early stages of their clinical studies.

Hence, could bulls have been too optimistic about the company's ability to level up its currently sole commercial approval rapidly across the next five years to meet Wall Street's optimistic prognosis?

Buy Or Sell CRSP Stock?

To be clear, let's not rejoice too early, as the top in July 2025 could also mark a pivotal bearish turning point in CRSP's previous surge that could still precede a more substantive selling wave.

We must consider that CRSP is expected to stay unprofitable from a free cash flow perspective through 2029. Hence, based on its current forward revenue multiple of 57x (well above the biotech median of 3x), it's clear that investors are betting on its ability to achieve massive success as it scales its gene editing tech.

Yet, please do not forget that CRSP's highly volatile stock is susceptible to downward reversals when buying sentiments betting on growth catalysts become exhausted. A timely reminder of the debilitating reversal after Casgevy first got its commercial approval in late 2023 should not be so quickly discarded.

Therefore, even if I were to give the benefit of the doubt to CRISPR's capabilities to make constructive progress over the next year, I'm not sure whether it might be enough to justify its already steep premium. Hence, I assess that the opportunity for investors to cut exposure at a high seems even better than back in my previous article. Try not to wait for the rest of the market to start selling aggressively first before you make your move.

Rating: Maintain Sell.

data.title: General Mills: Undervalued Stock For Income-Leaning Dividend Growth Investors
data.description:
General Mills, Inc. (NYSE:GIS) is an American multinational manufacturer and marketer of branded, packaged consumer foods and pet foods. Founded in 1928 (but with roots dating back to 1856), General Mills is now a $27 billion (by market cap) food giant that employs more than 30,000 people.

The company reports revenue across four operating segments: North America Retail, 63% of FY 2024 sales; International, 14%; Pet, 12%; and North America Foodservice, 11%. As you can see, the vast majority of the company’s revenue is derived from North America. By product category, Snacks are the company’s largest source of sales (making up about 22% of annual revenue), while Cereal (16%) and Pet (12%) are also major contributors.

On one hand, General Mills is in a tough spot. It’s a purveyor of packaged and processed foods, which have been out of favor for years. Evolving consumer tastes, trends, and behaviors have tilted the tables toward newer, more agile businesses in the space that provide healthier and more natural options in the marketplace.

Cereal, for instance, has been steadily declining in popularity as a breakfast food (per Euromonitor, cereal market volumes have shrunk roughly two percentage points per year over the past decade) as healthier, protein-forward options have come to the forefront.

On the other hand, the company is still a very profitable enterprise, commanding a range of extremely successful, well-known brands such as Cheerios, Häagen-Dazs (in certain markets), Nature Valley, and Pillsbury. With more than 100 brands in 100 countries, General Mills is one of the world’s foremost packaged food companies, giving it scale, breadth, and leadership that few can rival.

In addition, through a series of acquisitions, the company has pivoted to pet foods, which is an attractive area to be in due to growing ownership and “humanization” of pets. The company’s Blue Buffalo brand, which is one of the top 10 pet food brands in the US, is especially formidable in this space.

Look, people have to eat. And while some people would, in a perfect world, prefer to eat healthier meals, these meals can oftentimes be more expensive and time-consuming to acquire and/or prepare. This makes cheaper, more convenient food options an easy choice to make for many consumers in many real-life scenarios.

This is particularly true in the current environment, where high inflation over the last several years has caused food prices to spike, pressuring consumers’ wallets and making cheaper food alternatives more alluring on a relative basis (even if these cheaper options aren’t always healthier choices).

General Mills isn’t growing super quickly. But it is a stable company that’s been around for more than a century, and there’s no reason to believe it won’t be around for another century. Along the way, it should continue to consistently grow its revenue, profit, and dividend.

Dividend Growth, Growth Rate, Payout Ratio and Yield

To that point, General Mills has already increased its dividend for six consecutive years.

Its dividend growth track record would be much more impressive if not for the company’s decision to keep its dividend static for a few years after acquiring Blue Buffalo (an ~$8 billion acquisition which was mostly funded by debt) in order to better manage its cash flow and debt.

The company hasn’t always been super consistent when it comes to dividend increases, but it has been an ultra-reliable dividend star for nearly 130 years: General Mills and its predecessor company have been paying uninterrupted dividends since 1898.

For dividend lovers, that’s music to the ear. The temporary halt to dividend growth (but not to steady dividend payments) was, arguably, for the better, putting the company on better long-term financial footing.

Putting that aside, the three-year dividend growth rate of 5.3% is fairly solid. No, it’s not lighting the world on fire. However, this is getting layered on top of the stock’s market-smashing yield of 4.9%. That yield is in territory usually reserved for yield plays such as REITs and utilities. It’s pretty surprising to see a staid food company competing with REITs on yield.

For better perspective, this yield is 140 basis points higher than its own five-year average. An incredible spread. And with the payout ratio sitting at 58%, this dividend looks quite safe. The yield being so unusually high makes this a compelling option for income-seeking investors who aren’t necessarily in the market for fast growth.

Many 5% yields out there come with even lower growth than this, so one isn’t really making some big sacrifice here. I think this is one of the better and more sustainable 5% yields in the market.

Revenue and Earnings Growth

As much as that may be true, though, these metrics are mainly based on what’s already happened in the past. However, investors must always be thinking about what could happen in the future, as today’s capital is risked for tomorrow’s rewards. As such, I’ll now build out a forward-looking growth trajectory for the business, which will be of use when estimating fair value.

I’ll first show you what the business has done over the last decade in terms of its top-line and bottom-line growth. I’ll then reveal a professional prognostication for near-term profit growth. Amalgamating the proven past with a future forecast in this way should give us what we need to roughly judge where the business might be going from here.

General Mills grew its revenue from $16.6 billion in FY 2016 to $19.5 billion in FY 2025. That’s a compound annual growth rate of 1.8%. Somewhat disappointing, although this isn’t shocking. We’re talking about a mature, staid company fighting a secular shift away from packaged, processed foods. What’s a bit disappointing here, though, is the fact that this even includes the large Blue Buffalo acquisition, meaning we’d be looking at an even worse figure if not for the foray into pet foods.

Meantime, earnings per share increased from $2.77 to $4.10 over this period, which is a CAGR of 4.5%. A combination of buybacks and margin expansion helped to drive excess bottom-line growth. Regarding the former, the outstanding share count has been reduced by ~9% over the last decade.

Expanding margins and steady buybacks indicate to me that management has done an admirable job of steering the company in the face of challenging industry trends and macroeconomics. It’s not like management is resting on their laurels and/or actively trying to ruin the company. There have been incremental changes in the portfolio, along with acquisitions designed to better suit the marketplace.

Looking forward, CFRA believes that General Mills will put up a -4% CAGR in EPS over the next three years. Regarding the pessimism, CFRA cites: “…weak organic growth across key categories due to value-seeking consumers, shifting consumer habits, and heightened competition from branded players and private labels.” I don’t think CFRA is totally out of line here.

General Mills most recently reported a 2% decrease in revenue and a 5% decrease in EPS for Q4 FY 2025. A tumultuous period during the pandemic, which resulted in lumpy demand and high inflation/input costs, led companies like General Mills to sacrifice volumes in order to take price and maintain margins. If there is a knock against management, it may be the long-term give on volumes in order to take short-term gains on pricing.

In my view, this was a mistake that gave private labels an advantage (which may be permanent in nature). Things are still in the process of normalizing for General Mills, and Q4’s numbers were an improvement showing the company moving in the right direction, but we’re not totally there yet. A continued slow descent into deterioration and mediocrity over the coming years would be undoubtedly a disappointing outcome for shareholders, even with the dividend.

After all, 4% shrinkage is barely offset by a 5% yield, and the stock’s 1.9% CAGR (including reinvested dividends) over the last decade is quite poor and goes to show the importance of total return. Those who have held this stock over the last decade have little to show for their patience and would have done much better had they instead held shares in any number of higher-quality businesses.

Of course, that’s the past. What matters now is the future. On this front, I’m not quite as negative as CFRA, and I do think a normalization process, which is clearly playing out in the numbers, gives us something to be optimistic about directionally.

All General Mills has to do is grow the business at a low single-digit rate (which opens the dividend up for like-or-better growth) in order to be a satisfactory investment from here. It’s not a high hurdle to clear. As Warren Buffett might say, this is a one-foot bar to step over.

General Mills increased its dividend by 1.7% in June, and this is during a time which is about as bad as it gets for the company. Even modest improvement can get the dividend growth back into a mid-single-digit range, which would be more than enough to make sense of a stock already yielding 5%. If one leans toward yield/income, this all adds up to something pretty interesting.

Financial Position

Moving over to the balance sheet, General Mills has a decent financial position. The long-term debt/equity ratio is 1.4, while the interest coverage ratio is just over 6. These numbers aren’t awful, but they’re also nothing to write home about. While General Mills has never (for as long as I’ve been investing) had anything close to a pristine balance sheet, its financial position has weakened in recent years (most notably from the large Blue Buffalo acquisition).

I’d like to see some improvement here, but industry dynamics will likely limit any possibility of material movement. Profitability is fairly robust. Return on equity has averaged 25.7% over the last five years, while net margin has averaged 12.9%. Surprisingly strong numbers. ROE is juiced by the balance sheet, but even ROIC is routinely in a low-double-digit range.

General Mills has seen better days, but it remains a top-tier company in its space (as challenged as that space may be). And with economies of scale, brand/pricing power, established retail shelf space, and a global distribution network, the company does benefit from durable competitive advantages.

Of course, there are risks to consider. Litigation, regulation, and competition are omnipresent risks in every industry. Competition, in particular, is a problem – a multifaceted problem, no less – as General Mills faces competition from traditional players, retailers (via private-label products), and newer entrants resonating with younger consumers.

Adding on to that prior risk factor, the company’s major direct customers (i.e., retailers) have increasingly become competitors (by selling their own private-label products). There is a secular shift away from a large portion of the company’s products. The current US presidential administration has apparently taken a harsh stance toward packaged/processed foods, which is a headwind for General Mills.

Recent price taking has hurt volumes, and this short-term gain for long-term pain may be permanent for General Mills. The stretched balance sheet limits M&A opportunities, which has recently been one of the only ways in which General Mills has been able to adapt to changing trends. Input costs vary.

Being a global company, General Mills is exposed to currency exchange rates, different/changing tastes across cultures, and geopolitics. The rise of GLP-1s may accelerate the secular shift away from processed foods. I definitely see some risks to contend with.

However, the market has already sniffed these risks out and reassigned the stock an extremely low valuation after a 45% drop in the stock’s price since May 2023…

Valuation

The P/E ratio is 12.1. Yes, just 12.1. Even for a company that’s seen better days, that is shockingly low (especially in this market). To be fair, this stock has rarely commanded high multiples, but this current P/E ratio is well below its own five-year average of 15.8. The P/CF ratio of 10.2, which is very undemanding, is also far lower than its own five-year average of 12.9. And the yield, as noted earlier, is significantly higher than its own recent historical average.

So the stock looks cheap when looking at basic valuation metrics. But how cheap might it be? What would a rational estimate of intrinsic value look like?

I valued shares using a dividend discount model analysis. I factored in a 10% discount rate and a long-term dividend growth rate of 5%. I’m basically assuming that General Mills can get back to something it’s historically been close to. This would only require low single-digit top-line growth, upon which General Mills can pull some levers (such as buybacks and margin expansion) to get EPS to a mid-single-digit type of growth rate.

That would allow the dividend to cruise along at a 5% or so growth rate. There’s nothing herculean about that, but General Mills will have to tweak the portfolio and continue to respond to changing dynamics across the marketplace. The DDM analysis gives me a fair value of $51.24.

The reason I use a dividend discount model analysis is because a business is ultimately equal to the sum of all the future cash flow it can provide. The DDM analysis is a tailored version of the discounted cash flow model analysis, as it simply substitutes dividends and dividend growth for cash flow and growth.

It then discounts those future dividends back to the present day, to account for the time value of money since a dollar tomorrow is not worth the same amount as a dollar today. I find it to be a fairly accurate way to value dividend growth stocks.

From my vantage point, the stock looks slightly better than fairly valued after a heavy beatdown and revaluation by the market. But we’ll now compare that valuation with where two professional stock analysis firms have come out at. This adds balance, depth, and perspective to our conclusion.

Morningstar, a leading and well-respected stock analysis firm, rates stocks on a 5-star system. 1 star would mean a stock is substantially overvalued; 5 stars would mean a stock is substantially undervalued. 3 stars would indicate roughly fair value. Morningstar rates GIS as a 4-star stock, with a fair value estimate of $62.00.

CFRA is another professional analysis firm, and I like to compare my valuation opinion to theirs to see if I’m out of line. They similarly rate stocks on a 1-5 star scale, with 1 star meaning a stock is a strong sell and 5 stars meaning a stock is a strong buy. 3 stars is a hold. CFRA rates GIS as a 3-star “HOLD”, with a 12-month target price of $55.00.

I’m on the low end this time around. Averaging the three numbers out gives us a final valuation of $56.08, which would indicate the stock is possibly 11% undervalued.

Bottom line: General Mills, Inc., with more than 100 brands in more than 100 countries, is one of the world’s foremost packaged food companies. Despite challenging dynamics in its industry, General Mills has scale, breadth, and leadership that few can rival. With a market-smashing yield, mid-single-digit dividend growth, a reasonable payout ratio, more than five consecutive years of dividend increases, and the potential that shares are 11% undervalued, income-leaning, value-seeking dividend growth investors have a cheap, high-yield stock on their hands here.

Note from D&I: How safe is GIS’s dividend? We ran the stock through Simply Safe Dividends, and as we go to press, its Dividend Safety Score is 90. Dividend Safety Scores range from 0 to 100. A score of 50 is average, 75 or higher is excellent, and 25 or lower is weak. With this in mind, GIS’s dividend appears Very Safe with a very unlikely risk of being cut.

Disclosure: I’m long GIS.
